card.title: Why Wedding Dresses Are White
card.teaser: For most of human history, brides wore their best dress in any color. Then a 20-year-old queen changed the rules in one afternoon.
card.coverImagePrompt: A regal Victorian-era oil portrait of a young bride in a pure white satin gown with long lace veil, standing in a cathedral nave, soft stained-glass light

lessons.title: The dress that started it all
lessons.body: On February 10, 1840, Queen Victoria walked down the aisle to marry Prince Albert in a white silk-satin gown trimmed with handmade Honiton lace. Nobody had asked her to wear white — it wasn't tradition, and most brides still wore their best colored dress. Newspapers worldwide gasped. Within a decade, white had become aspirational for any bride with the means to afford it.

visual.imagePrompt: A black-and-white 1840s engraving of Queen Victoria in her wedding gown, crowd of royals in color behind her, crisp ink detail

lessons.title: Why white used to be a terrible choice
lessons.body: Before cheap washing machines and modern detergents, white fabric was a nightmare. It stained instantly, showed every smudge, and needed hours of hand-washing to stay clean. For most women, a wedding dress was also their best dress for years afterward — so they picked practical colors: blues, browns, greens, even black. A white gown you'd wear once was pure extravagance.

keyTerms.definition: A woman's finest garment, typically worn for many special occasions including her wedding
The clothes, linens, and household goods a bride brought into marriage

visual.imagePrompt: A 1830s washerwoman scrubbing white lace over a wooden basin, soap bubbles catching afternoon light, moody Dutch-painter tone

lessons.title: What white actually meant to Victoria
lessons.body: Victoria's choice wasn't about virginity — that myth came later. She chose white partly to show off the struggling Honiton lace industry in Devon, which was losing work to machine-made imports. The white silk let the lace stand out. It was a political statement wrapped in a fashion statement: a young queen using her wedding to boost British handcraft exports.

visual.imagePrompt: Close-up of intricate handmade Honiton lace being pinned on a gown form, skilled hands, soft window light, craft documentary style

lessons.title: Purity came later
lessons.body: The link between white dresses and virginity became prominent only in the late Victorian era, when fashion magazines began marketing white as the color of 'innocence'. A now-infamous 1849 column in Godey's Lady's Book claimed white had 'from the remotest ages' been 'the fittest hue' — which was simply false. The 'ancient tradition' was barely a decade old.
lessons.funFact: Victoria's own daughters mostly wore gold or silver for their weddings — the strict white rule became a middle-class obsession long after the royals had moved on.

visual.imagePrompt: A 19th-century women's fashion magazine page illustrating an idealized white-clad bride, ornate borders, sepia tones

lessons.title: A very modern tradition
lessons.body: The white wedding dress is sometimes presented as timeless. It isn't. It's a mid-19th-century invention that became affordable mass tradition only after electric washing machines, commercial dry-cleaning, and rising middle-class incomes made one-use finery thinkable. Most 'ancient wedding traditions' are about 150 years old — which is about as deep as most traditions ever really go.

quiz.question: Whose 1840 wedding popularized the white dress?
quiz.explanation: Victoria's white silk-satin gown, trimmed with Honiton lace, launched the fashion worldwide.

quiz.question: Before 1840, what color was a typical Western bride's wedding dress?
options: Whatever her best dress happened to be
quiz.explanation: Brides typically wore their finest existing dress, which was most often a practical color like blue, brown, or green.

quiz.question: Why did Victoria specifically choose white?
options: To showcase and support the British Honiton lace industry
Because no other color was available
quiz.explanation: White let the handmade Honiton lace stand out — a deliberate boost to a British craft industry in decline.
